opp.name: Squarespace [Spend/ANA] April'26
opp.next_step: Schedule a Follow-Up Call : Action : Coordinate a 15-minute call between Ilysa and the Sales Consultant (SC) to further discuss her specific needs, the benefits of Spend Analytics , and ANA Goal : Clarify any questions she may have about the solution and finalize the next steps for the demo session . Schedule Live Demo : Action : After the follow-up call , set up a live demo of Zycus Spend Analytics and Anna for Ilysa and her team. Goal : Demonstrate how Spend Analytics can provide real-time spend insights, and show the value of Anna in automating negotiations and delivering cost savings. Offer Pilot Program : Action : Discuss a complimentary 4-week pilot of Anna during the follow-up call or demo session. This will help Ilysa visualize the potential savings (2-3% tail spend savings) and understand the impact of automation. Goal : Give Ilysa a hands-on experience of how Anna works in practice and demonstrate the quick wins in tail spend management. Key Discussion Points : Spend Analytics : Ilysa expressed interest in the capabilities of Zycus' Spend Analytics and its AI-driven insights, especially regarding tail spend management . Autonomous Negotiation (Anna) : We discussed the potential savings (2-3%) from Anna , Zycus’ AI-powered tool for automating supplier negotiations. She seemed intrigued by the savings potential for non-strategic procurement. Pain Points : Squarespace is looking for better visibility into procurement spend, with a focus on non-strategic purchases (tail spend). Coupa is currently being used for PO/invoicing, but there’s room for improvement in sourcing and negotiation processes .
opp.ais_why:
• The opportunity name carries 'ANA' (Autonomous Negotiation) and the Merlin product line includes ANA and Merlin, but the AI framing is vendor-driven. Zycus rates AI importance as 'Important' for the account, however the agentic-platform discussion, Gen AI commentary and AI-RFP rating fields are all empty, so no buyer-stated ANA or Merlin requirements have been captured.
• The May 1 discovery with Ilysa Laulom was logistics-only. The action items were to assign a solution consultant, send dates for a 15-minute prep call, and set up the pilot conversation. AI, Merlin, agentic and autonomous negotiation did not come up from the buyer in any captured action item.
• The opp was created in May 2026 and only two of four meetings are completed. The substantive sessions are short prep and demo-planning calls. With no buyer-voiced AI evidence yet and the ANA tag in the name being Zycus-side framing, this is genuinely early. There is room to move up after the first real demo, but today there is nothing to anchor a higher score.

sources: Ilysa Laulom, 01 May 2026 discovery call: 'we manage about $500,000,000 in spend... this would be a net new thing for us... it's all manual'
Ilysa Laulom, 11 May 2026 scoping call: 'it's all manual today... identifying those opportunities to consolidate spend and identify savings'
Edward Dlugosz, 01 May 2026 call: 'tail-spend savings between 3-23% easily... pilot targeting 3-5% average savings'
SF Business_Objectives__c, Customer_Business_Problem__c, Value_to_Customer__c fields not populated
metrics.narrative: Ilysa Laulom (Head of Procurement) stated on the 01 May discovery call that Squarespace manages approximately $500M in spend and currently performs all spend analysis and consolidation opportunity identification manually, calling it 'all manual today' (11 May call). She articulated the operational pain as lack of visibility and the need to identify 'consolidation opportunities and what types of things we're buying' without a dedicated system. On the 01 May call, Edward positioned the ANA pilot as targeting '3-5% average savings on tail spend' over a 4-week pilot covering 3-5 categories, and Ilysa confirmed interest. However, no quantified business case (cost of manual work, error rate, cycle time, or ROI target) was stated by the buyer across any call. The pain is confirmed and operational (manual, immature taxonomy, no spend visibility), but the value case remains qualitative. The next move is to co-build a quantified savings model with Ilysa and the finance systems team, tying the manual effort cost and the 3-5% tail-spend savings to an ROI Squarespace finance can sponsor.

SF X10b_Champion_Business_Buyer__c empty; no Champion contact role
champion.narrative: Ilysa Laulom (Head of Procurement, email ilaulom@squarespace.com) is the primary contact and a developing champion. She initiated the engagement (scheduled the 28 Apr intro call, rescheduled to 01 May), led all four buyer calls, expressed prior positive experience with Zycus ('I have used Zycus before... I used Spanalyze a long time ago at Federal Home Loan Bank in San Francisco', 01 May), and requested both the spend analytics and ANA demos ('I'd love to also see a demo of Anna', 01 May; 'I've never seen Anna before, it just sounds interesting', 11 May). She brought in the financial systems team (Noel Slater, Rajiv) for the 19 May demo, showing cross-functional pull, and closed the demo with 'it was helpful, we'll regroup internally and reach out', signaling intent to advance. However, she has not replied in 38 days since 19 May, has not introduced Edward/Jordan to the Economic Buyer or CFO, and has not confirmed the pilot or next step. Her engagement is active through 19 May but has gone quiet since. She is a champion in terms of access (she owns the relationship) and prior affinity (used Zycus 20 years ago), but her advocacy is unproven—no evidence she is selling internally, shielding Zycus from competitors, or driving urgency. The risk is that she is a coach (helpful, engaged) but not a champion (selling for us when we are not in the room). The next move is for Edward to re-engage her with a high-value artifact (a draft ROI model or a Squarespace-specific consolidation-savings estimate based on the $500M spend) and ask her directly: 'What would help you build the business case internally, and who else should we bring into the conversation to move this forward?'—testing whether she will advocate or deflect.

sources: Ilysa Laulom, 01 May 2026: 'we use Coupa for PO invoicing processing and we use Zip for intake'
Ilysa Laulom, 11 May 2026: 'we use Coupa and Zip... flows from Zip, then we use Coupa to create the POs'
Buyer, 19 May 2026 demo: 'how is this better than us pulling a report from Coupa with our top 5?'
Edward Dlugosz, 01 May: 'Coupa and Ariba bolt on modules, Zycus unified database and codebase'
SF Competitors__c, Others_Competitors_Please_specify__c, Existing_vendor__c all empty; no formal competitor logged
competition.narrative: Coupa is the incumbent for PO/invoicing and the primary competitive threat; Zip is the current intake tool, a secondary/adjacent player. Ilysa stated on 01 May 'we use Coupa for PO invoicing processing and we use Zip for intake', and on 11 May clarified 'we use Coupa and Zip for intake... flows from Zip for intake information, then we use Coupa to create the POs... they have to go in and approve invoices in Coupa.' Coupa is live and operational; Squarespace is seeking a net-new spend analytics solution to layer on top, not a rip-and-replace. Edward positioned Zycus as differentiated from Coupa on the 01 May call: 'Coupa and Ariba bolt on modules, Zycus applications share a unified database and codebase', and noted that Coupa does not offer the Merlin intake Teams interface ('we interface through Teams, Coupa uses a web interface'). On the 19 May demo, a buyer (likely Noel or Rajiv, voice unclear) asked 'how is this better than us pulling a report from Coupa with our top 5?', signaling that Coupa's native reporting is the bar to beat. No other competitor (Ariba, Ivalua, Jaggaer, GEP) has been named. The competitive wedge is that Zycus offers purpose-built AI-driven spend analytics (AutoClass, iAnalyze, Spendminer, Merlin Insights) and ANA (autonomous tail-spend negotiation) that Coupa does not natively provide, and can integrate with Coupa as a data source while delivering superior taxonomy refinement, consolidation-opportunity ID, and autonomous sourcing. The threat is low to medium: Coupa is not actively being evaluated for spend analytics (Ilysa said 'we don't have a system right now for spend analysis'), but the incumbent relationship and the 38-day silence suggest Squarespace may be evaluating a do-nothing or Coupa-native-reporting option. The activation is to quantify the gap: show Ilysa a side-by-side of what Coupa reporting delivers vs what Zycus iAnalyze + ANA delivers, and tie it to a 3-month savings outcome the CFO would fund.

sources: Ilysa Laulom, 01 May 2026: 'we don't have a system right now for spend analysis... net new... manage about $500,000,000 in spend... it's all manual'
Ilysa Laulom, 11 May 2026: 'we're pretty immature in this space... not categorizing suppliers... data is free form... nothing granular enough to run reports on'
Ilysa Laulom, 01 May 2026: 'I'd like to understand the value that spend analysis brings... right now it's all manual'
Pain owner: Ilysa Laulom, Head of Procurement, led all buyer calls and stated the need directly
identify_pain.narrative: Ilysa Laulom (Head of Procurement) owns the pain and articulated it clearly across multiple calls. On 01 May she said 'we don't have a system right now for spend analysis, so there's no switch... this would be a net new thing for us... we're just maturing our procurement organization, pretty small team, but manage about $500,000,000 in spend... right now it's all manual, the work that we're doing to see consolidation opportunities and what types of things we're buying.' On 11 May she expanded: 'we're pretty immature in this space, we're not categorizing suppliers, the data just feeds from an intake form that the requester fills out, a lot of it's free form... nothing granular enough for us to run reports on.' The operational pain is lack of spend visibility, immature/free-form data taxonomy, manual consolidation analysis, and inability to report at the granular software-category level needed for SaaS rationalization. The pain is confirmed, owned by Ilysa (Head of Procurement), and tied to the $500M spend base. No quantified cost of the manual work or error rate, but the qualitative pain is substantive and directly maps to the Zycus spend analytics and ANA value prop. What is missing is the compelling event or timeline driver—no urgency signal, no budget-year deadline, no exec mandate. The next move is to ask Ilysa what is driving the timeline and whether there is a forcing function (budget cycle, audit, exec directive) that would anchor the evaluation and close.

sources: No contracting, legal, InfoSec, or procurement-process discussion on any of the four buyer calls (01 May, 11 May, 19 May)
SF Current_Contract_Expiration__c empty; no forcing function
SF What_is_the_decision_process__c, Mandate__c, Does_the_Buyer_need_approval__c all empty
paper_process.narrative: No MSA, SOW, legal, InfoSec, or procurement-process discussion on any call. No contract expiration forcing function (SF Current_Contract_Expiration__c empty). No mention of Squarespace's contracting mechanics, legal review, or InfoSec requirements across any of the four buyer calls. Rajiv and Noel (financial systems) attended the 19 May demo but focused on technical integration and taxonomy, not procurement/legal process. No statement of whether Squarespace requires an RFP, a pilot agreement, or a direct SOW, and no timeline for legal/InfoSec review. The deal is new (created 01 May 2026) and early-stage, so the absence of paper-process discussion is appropriate for the phase, but it is unknown. The next move is for Edward to ask Ilysa on the re-engagement call: 'If we proceed to pilot and then purchase, what does your contracting process look like—do you require an RFP response, a pilot SOW, InfoSec review, legal redlines—and what is the typical cycle time for each step?' and map that into the mutual close plan.

economic_buyer.narrative: No Economic Buyer or budget authority identified across any call or Salesforce field. Ilysa Laulom (Head of Procurement) led all four buyer calls and is the primary contact, but no pricing, contract, or budget conversation occurred on any call—all were discovery and demo focused. No CFO, VP Finance, or C-level exec joined any call. The 19 May demo included Noel Slater (Financial Systems Architect) and Rajiv (Financial Systems lead), both technical/operational roles with no stated budget or commercial authority. SF fields X10a_Sponsor__c, Who_will_approve_budget__c, Purchase_approvals_Required_from__c, Does_the_Buyer_need_approval__c are all empty, and there is no Economic Buyer or Executive Sponsor contact role. The org chart shows Nathan Gooden (CFO) and Marcela Martin (Chief Financial Officer) as account-level contacts, but neither has appeared on a call or in correspondence. The deal is 11 months from close with no EB mapped; the next move is for Edward and Ilysa to schedule a 30-minute session to map the approval chain, identify who controls the $300k budget, and confirm the path to CFO/exec sign-off, then secure an EB intro call.

sources: Call sequence: 28 Apr (aborted), 01 May (discovery), 11 May (scoping), 19 May (demo with financial systems team)
Ilysa Laulom, 19 May demo: 'we'll regroup internally as a team and then reach out'
Edward Dlugosz, 19 May demo: 'I'll reach out if I don't hear from you in a week or two'
38 days no buyer reply (last activity 19 May, today 26 Jun)
SF What_is_the_decision_process__c, Mandate__c, Does_the_Buyer_need_approval__c all empty
01 May call: 4-week ANA pilot discussed but no pilot agreement or start date confirmed
decision_process.narrative: The call sequence shows a discovery and demo cycle: (1) aborted intro call 28 Apr, (2) successful discovery 01 May (Ilysa + Edward + Vinay), (3) scoping call 11 May (Ilysa + Edward + Jordan), (4) full demo 19 May (Ilysa + Noel Slater + Rajiv + Edward + Jordan). The 19 May demo brought in the financial systems team (Noel, Rajiv), signaling that technical/data-architecture buy-in is part of the evaluation. Ilysa said at the end of the 19 May demo 'we'll regroup internally as a team and then reach out to you guys if we have any questions or regarding next steps', and Edward replied 'I'll reach out to you if I don't hear from you in a week or two.' No buyer reply in 38 days suggests the internal regroup has stalled or deprioritized. No formal approval chain, procurement committee, or decision timeline has been stated. SF fields What_is_the_decision_process__c, Mandate__c, Does_the_Buyer_need_approval__c, Multiple_approvals__c are all empty. The 4-week ANA pilot was discussed on 01 May ('complimentary 4-week pilot... weeks 2-3 testing, week 4 live use') but no pilot agreement or start date was confirmed. The close date of 31 May 2027 (11 months out) implies a long evaluation, but no milestones (pilot kick-off, RFP response, EB review, contract negotiation) have been mapped. The next move is for Edward to re-engage Ilysa with a proposed decision timeline and mutual close plan: pilot kick-off, pilot review, EB intro, contract/legal, and close by Q1 2027, and ask her to validate or correct it.

sources: Ilysa Laulom, 01 May call: 'the normalization is key... I'm interested in all of the above, the normalization and then the recommendations'
Ilysa Laulom, 11 May call: 'identifying those opportunities to consolidate spend and identify savings'
Ilysa Laulom, 19 May demo: 'can you run a report showing types of software or software category'
Noel Slater, 19 May demo: detailed taxonomy, integration, and data governance questions
Rajiv, 19 May demo: 'are these reports out of the box, or did we have to develop with you as part of implementation'
SF Business_Requirements__c, Top_Challenges_Priorities__c, AI_Needs_in_RFP_Rating__c all empty; no formal RFP or BRD shared
decision_criteria.narrative: Ilysa stated clear functional requirements across the 01 May and 11 May calls: (1) data normalization and taxonomy refinement ('the normalization is key, and Zycus can do that', 01 May), (2) automated identification of consolidation opportunities and savings recommendations ('I'm interested in seeing both', 01 May; 'identifying those opportunities to consolidate spend', 11 May), (3) granular software-category reporting ('can you run a report showing types of software or software category, like a cost of that', 19 May demo), and (4) integration with Coupa (PO/invoicing) and Zip (intake). Noel Slater and Rajiv on the 19 May demo asked detailed questions about taxonomy master data management, integration architecture (REST APIs, SFTP), and whether dashboards/reports are out-of-the-box or custom-built during implementation, signaling evaluation criteria around configurability, data governance, and ERP/Coupa sync. However, no formal RFP, BRD, scorecard, or weighted criteria have been shared, and no statement of 'must-haves vs nice-to-haves' or competitive evaluation framework. The criteria are inferred from buyer questions and stated needs, not confirmed as the decision rubric. The next move is for Jordan (SC) to send Ilysa a draft decision-criteria summary based on the 19 May demo Q&A and ask her to validate and weight it, then confirm whether Squarespace will run a formal RFP or a phased pilot-to-purchase path.

ai_fit_signal.summary: Ilysa expressed interest in AI capabilities ('there's been a lot of changes with AI and all that fun stuff, so really interested in getting a demo of spend analytics', 01 May; 'I've never seen Anna before, it just sounds interesting', 11 May) and the financial systems team engaged positively on AI-driven taxonomy (AutoClass) and insights (Merlin). However, no strategic AI initiative, no exec mandate to adopt AI procurement tools, and no quantified AI ROI target stated. The AI interest is curiosity-driven (AI Curious), not mandate-driven (AI Hungry). No AIS score in Salesforce to calibrate.

items.trigger: No decision process, timeline, or milestones mapped; close date 31 May 2027 is 11 months out with no interim gates; Ilysa said 'we'll regroup internally' but no plan shared
items.expected_effect: Create a shared roadmap and accountability; a mutual close plan forces Ilysa to state the decision process, approval gates, and timeline; if she cannot validate it or pushes back, it surfaces the lack of urgency or organizational readiness and allows Edward to address it directly; if she accepts, it re-anchors the deal to a committed path forward

items: Positive: Ilysa Laulom is a prior Zycus user (Spanalyze 20 years ago) and led all four buyer calls, showing ownership and access
Positive: Financial systems team (Noel Slater, Rajiv) engaged on 19 May demo with detailed technical questions and no blocking concerns
Positive: Strong functional fit on all stated requirements (taxonomy, consolidation, granular reporting, Coupa integration, ANA tail-spend automation)
Negative: 38-day silence since 19 May demo despite Ilysa's commitment to 'regroup internally and reach out' and Edward's 1-2 week follow-up promise
Negative: No Economic Buyer, CFO, or C-level exec identified or engaged across any call or Salesforce field
Negative: No compelling event, urgency driver, or decision timeline stated; close date 11 months out with no interim milestones
Negative: No pilot SOW, data-share agreement, or kick-off date confirmed despite pilot discussion on 01 May
Negative: Single-threaded through Ilysa; 0 buyer-side contact roles on opp despite 3 buyer participants on 19 May demo
confidence_signals.summary: Positive early engagement (discovery + demo cycle 01-19 May, multi-stakeholder demo, prior Zycus affinity) but 38-day cooling period and lack of EB/CFO engagement are strong negative signals; deal is at risk of going fully dark or being deprioritized

north_star_verdict.math: Created 01 May 2026, 56 days old; stage Formal Evaluation; close date 31 May 2027 (339 days out, 11 months). Last verified buyer activity 19 May 2026 (38 days ago, full demo with Ilysa, Noel Slater, Rajiv on spend analytics and ANA). No buyer engagement since; NextStep notes a planned 15-minute SC call and demo scheduling but no evidence it occurred. Cooling for 38 days in Formal Evaluation with no demo follow-up or next-step confirmation is a loss of momentum. Close date is distant so no immediate timing pressure, but 38-day silence after a live multi-stakeholder demo is a stall. Stage velocity unknown (no Qualified_Submission_Date__c or Formal_Eval_Submission_Date__c). Forecast Pipeline is appropriate given the early stage and long runway, but the 38-day gap is a red flag that the deal is losing heat.
evidence: Last buyer call 19 May 2026 (38 days ago): full spend analytics and ANA demo with Ilysa Laulom, Noel Slater (Financial Systems Architect), and Rajiv (Financial Systems lead)
NextStep (last modified 05 Jun 2026) notes a planned 15-minute SC prep call and live demo scheduling, but no evidence of buyer reply or follow-up activity since 19 May
No new tasks, emails, or buyer-facing activity logged after 19 May (LastActivityDate 19 May)
Demo on 19 May was substantive: Ilysa said 'it was helpful, we'll regroup internally and reach out'; Ed committed to follow up if no reply in 1-2 weeks; 38 days later, no buyer re-engagement
Stage Formal Evaluation appropriate for the discovery/demo phase; Products ML iAnalyze;iSave;Merlin;ANA align with demo scope
Close date 31 May 2027 is 11 months out, so no immediate timing pressure, but 38-day silence after a live demo is a stall
Amount $300k; created 01 May 2026, 56 days old; opp is new but the engagement has already cooled
north_star_verdict.headline: Strong 19 May demo with three stakeholders went dark for 38 days. Buyer said 'we'll regroup and reach out'—no follow-through despite rep outreach. Formal Evaluation stalled; risk of do-nothing drift against Coupa incumbent reporting.

best_practice_check.summary: Strong functional fit and early positive engagement (discovery + demo cycle 01-19 May) but 38-day cooling period signals loss of momentum; needs immediate re-activation with high-value artifact (ROI model or savings estimate) and EB/CFO intro to restart the cycle before the deal goes fully dark

competitive_position.summary: Coupa is the live incumbent for PO/invoicing and the primary threat; Zip is the current intake tool. Squarespace is seeking a net-new spend analytics solution to layer on top of Coupa, not a rip-and-replace. No active competitive evaluation named, but the buyer's 19 May question 'how is this better than pulling a report from Coupa?' signals Coupa native reporting is the do-nothing alternative. Threat level medium: Coupa is not being evaluated for spend analytics but the incumbent relationship and 38-day silence suggest a do-nothing or Coupa-sufficient mindset may be forming. Zycus wedge: purpose-built AI spend analytics (AutoClass, iAnalyze, Merlin Insights) and ANA autonomous tail-spend negotiation that Coupa does not offer, with Coupa integration as data source. Activation: quantify the Coupa-reporting gap vs Zycus value in a side-by-side and tie to 3-month savings outcome.

competitors.name: Coupa
competitors.quote: We use Coupa for PO invoicing processing and we use Zip for intake (Ilysa, 01 May). Buyer on 19 May demo asked 'how is this better than us pulling a report from Coupa with our top 5?'
competitors.source: Ilysa Laulom, 01 May and 11 May calls; buyer question on 19 May demo
competitors.how_we_win: Coupa is PO/invoicing, not purpose-built spend analytics; position Zycus as the AI-driven analytics + ANA layer on top of Coupa (not a replacement), delivering taxonomy refinement, consolidation-opportunity ID, and autonomous tail-spend sourcing Coupa cannot; quantify the reporting gap in a side-by-side and tie to a 3-month pilot savings outcome

competitors.name: Zip
competitors.quote: We use Zip for intake... it's a web interface (Ilysa, 01 May). Zip has intake module, not PO module (Ilysa, 11 May)
competitors.source: Ilysa Laulom, 01 May and 11 May calls
competitors.how_we_win: Zip is intake workflow, not spend analytics or sourcing; not a direct competitor for the Zycus iAnalyze + ANA scope; can integrate as data source if needed

competitors.name: Do Nothing (manual spend analysis)
competitors.quote: Right now it's all manual, the work that we're doing to see consolidation opportunities (Ilysa, 01 May). 38-day silence since 19 May demo suggests buyer may be content with current state or Coupa native reporting
competitors.how_we_win: Quantify the cost of manual work and the opportunity cost of no spend visibility; position the 4-week ANA pilot as a low-risk, high-ROI proof point (3-5% tail-spend savings in 4 weeks); create urgency by tying to a budget-year or audit deadline if one exists

ai_positioning_strength.summary: Strong AI positioning: ANA (autonomous negotiation agent) and Merlin Insights were core to all three discovery/demo calls, Ilysa requested ANA demo twice ('I'd love to see a demo of Anna', 'I've never seen Anna before, it sounds interesting'), and the 19 May demo showcased AutoClass, iAnalyze, Merlin Insights, and ANA in depth. However, no AIS score in Salesforce and no statement from the buyer quantifying AI appetite or linking AI to their strategic objectives. The AI story landed as a feature (interesting, novel) but not as a strategic differentiator tied to Squarespace's business case. Merlin/ANA positioning is feature-strong but business-case-weak.

items.quote: The normalization is key, and Zycus can do that... I'm interested in all of the above, the normalization and then the recommendations (Ilysa, 01 May). We're pretty immature, not categorizing suppliers, data is free form, nothing granular enough to run reports (Ilysa, 11 May)
items.source: Ilysa Laulom, 01 May and 11 May discovery calls
items.criterion: Data normalization and taxonomy refinement at granular (level 3-4) category depth

items.quote: I'm interested in seeing both... identifying those opportunities to consolidate spend and identify savings (Ilysa, 01 and 11 May)
items.source: Ilysa Laulom, 01 May and 11 May calls
items.criterion: Automated identification of consolidation opportunities and savings recommendations

items.quote: Can you run a report showing types of software or software category, like a cost of that? (Ilysa, 19 May demo). Jordan demonstrated category-level drill-down reporting in iAnalyze; Ilysa confirmed interest
items.source: Ilysa Laulom and Jordan Weinberger, 19 May demo
items.criterion: Granular software-category reporting (e.g., 'types of software we're buying', cost per category)

items.quote: We use Coupa and Zip... data flows from Zip, then Coupa creates POs (Ilysa, 11 May). Jordan explained SFTP and REST API integration for Coupa and Zip on 19 May demo; Rajiv asked detailed integration questions, no blockers raised
items.source: Ilysa Laulom, 11 May; Rajiv and Jordan, 19 May demo
items.criterion: Integration with Coupa (PO/invoicing) and Zip (intake) as data sources

items.quote: Are these reports out of the box, or did we have to develop with you as part of implementation? (Rajiv, 19 May). Jordan confirmed standard reports out of the box, custom reports built collaboratively during implementation; Rajiv accepted
items.criterion: Out-of-the-box dashboards and reports vs custom build during implementation

items.quote: Spend under $2.5k... software... maybe security services, catering (Ilysa, 11 May). I'd love to see a demo of Anna (Ilysa, 01 May). I've never seen Anna before, it just sounds interesting (Ilysa, 11 May). Jordan demoed ANA autonomous sourcing on 19 May; no objections raised
items.source: Ilysa Laulom, 01 and 11 May; Jordan demo 19 May
items.criterion: Autonomous tail-spend sourcing (ANA) for spend under $2,500, software and indirect services categories

items.quote: No quantified cost of manual work, error rate, or ROI target stated by buyer on any call. Edward positioned 3-5% tail-spend savings on 01 May, but no buyer validation or business-case co-build. No EB or CFO on any call to validate budget or ROI threshold
items.source: All four buyer calls (01, 11, 19 May); SF Value_to_Customer__c empty
items.criterion: Quantified ROI and business case for CFO/EB sponsorship

customer_expectations_fit.summary: Strong functional fit on spend analytics requirements (taxonomy refinement, consolidation-opportunity ID, granular software-category reporting, Coupa/Zip integration); ANA tail-spend automation aligns with Ilysa's interest and the manual-process pain; financial systems team (Noel, Rajiv) engaged positively on technical architecture (APIs, SFTP, taxonomy master data). Gap: no quantified ROI target, no EB validation, no pilot agreement confirmed, and 38-day silence suggests organizational readiness or urgency may be lower than the functional fit implies.

hard.ais_why:
• The opportunity name carries 'ANA' (Autonomous Negotiation) and the Merlin product line includes ANA and Merlin, but the AI framing is vendor-driven. Zycus rates AI importance as 'Important' for the account, however the agentic-platform discussion, Gen AI commentary and AI-RFP rating fields are all empty, so no buyer-stated ANA or Merlin requirements have been captured.
• The May 1 discovery with Ilysa Laulom was logistics-only. The action items were to assign a solution consultant, send dates for a 15-minute prep call, and set up the pilot conversation. AI, Merlin, agentic and autonomous negotiation did not come up from the buyer in any captured action item.
• The opp was created in May 2026 and only two of four meetings are completed. The substantive sessions are short prep and demo-planning calls. With no buyer-voiced AI evidence yet and the ANA tag in the name being Zycus-side framing, this is genuinely early. There is room to move up after the first real demo, but today there is nothing to anchor a higher score.
hard.next_step:
• Schedule a Follow-Up Call:
    • Action: Coordinate a 15-minute call between Ilysa and the Sales Consultant (SC) to further discuss her specific needs, the benefits of Spend Analytics, and ANA
    • Goal: Clarify any questions she may have about the solution and finalize the next steps for the demo session.
  • Schedule Live Demo:
    • Action: After the follow-up call, set up a live demo of Zycus Spend Analytics and Anna for Ilysa and her team.
    • Goal: Demonstrate how Spend Analytics can provide real-time spend insights, and show the value of Anna in automating negotiations and delivering cost savings.
  • Offer Pilot Program:
    • Action: Discuss a complimentary 4-week pilot of Anna during the follow-up call or demo session. This will help Ilysa visualize the potential savings (2-3% tail spend savings) and understand the impact of automation.
    • Goal: Give Ilysa a hands-on experience of how Anna works in practice and demonstrate the quick wins in tail spend management.

 

Key Discussion Points:

  • Spend Analytics: Ilysa expressed interest in the capabilities of Zycus' Spend Analytics and its AI-driven insights, especially regarding tail spend management.
  • Autonomous Negotiation (Anna): We discussed the potential savings (2-3%) from Anna, Zycus’ AI-powered tool for automating supplier negotiations. She seemed intrigued by the savings potential for non-strategic procurement.
  • Pain Points: Squarespace is looking for better visibility into procurement spend, with a focus on non-strategic purchases (tail spend). Coupa is currently being used for PO/invoicing, but there’s room for improvement in sourcing and negotiation processes.

validation gate: hard.next_step 'Schedule a Follow-Up Call: Action: Coordinate a 15-minute call between Ilysa and the Sales Consultant (SC) to further discuss her specific needs, the benefits of Spend Analytics, and ANAGoal: Clarify any questions she may have about the solution and finalize the next steps for the demo session. Schedule Live Demo: Action: After the follow-up call, set up a live demo of Zycus Spend Analytics and Anna for Ilysa and her team.Goal: Demonstrate how Spend Analytics can provide real-time spend insights, and show the value of Anna in automating negotiations and delivering cost savings. Offer Pilot Program: Action: Discuss a complimentary 4-week pilot of Anna during the follow-up call or demo session. This will help Ilysa visualize the potential savings (2-3% tail spend savings) and understand the impact of automation.Goal: Give Ilysa a hands-on experience of how Anna works in practice and demonstrate the quick wins in tail spend management. Key Discussion Points: Spend Analytics: Ilysa expressed interest in the capabilities of Zycus' Spend Analytics and its AI-driven insights, especially regarding tail spend management.Autonomous Negotiation (Anna): We discussed the potential savings (2-3%) from Anna, Zycus’ AI-powered tool for automating supplier negotiations. She seemed intrigued by the savings potential for non-strategic procurement.Pain Points: Squarespace is looking for better visibility into procurement spend, with a focus on non-strategic purchases (tail spend). Coupa is currently being used for PO/invoicing, but there’s room for improvement in sourcing and negotiation processes.' diverges from Salesforce '
  • Schedule a Follow-Up Call:
    • Action: Coordinate a 15-minute call between Ilysa and the Sales Consultant (SC) to further discuss her specific needs, the benefits of Spend Analytics, and ANA
    • Goal: Clarify any questions she may have about the solution and finalize the next steps for the demo session.
  • Schedule Live Demo:
    • Action: After the follow-up call, set up a live demo of Zycus Spend Analytics and Anna for Ilysa and her team.
    • Goal: Demonstrate how Spend Analytics can provide real-time spend insights, and show the value of Anna in automating negotiations and delivering cost savings.
  • Offer Pilot Program:
    • Action: Discuss a complimentary 4-week pilot of Anna during the follow-up call or demo session. This will help Ilysa visualize the potential savings (2-3% tail spend savings) and understand the impact of automation.
    • Goal: Give Ilysa a hands-on experience of how Anna works in practice and demonstrate the quick wins in tail spend management.

\xa0

Key Discussion Points:

  • Spend Analytics: Ilysa expressed interest in the capabilities of Zycus' Spend Analytics and its AI-driven insights, especially regarding tail spend management.
  • Autonomous Negotiation (Anna): We discussed the potential savings (2-3%) from Anna, Zycus’ AI-powered tool for automating supplier negotiations. She seemed intrigued by the savings potential for non-strategic procurement.
  • Pain Points: Squarespace is looking for better visibility into procurement spend, with a focus on non-strategic purchases (tail spend). Coupa is currently being used for PO/invoicing, but there’s room for improvement in sourcing and negotiation processes.
